questions.text: What is the primary function of a switch in a network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To provide permanent physical links between all devices.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To create temporary connections between two or more linked devices.

choices.label: C
choices.text: To act as an end system for user communication.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To provide constant bandwidth to all nodes simultaneously.

questions.explanation: A switch is a device capable of creating temporary connections between two or more devices linked to it, allowing for the efficient transfer of data without requiring permanent dedicated links between every possible pair.

questions.text: In a switched network, nodes that are NOT connected to end systems are typically used for:
questions.explanation: Nodes in a switched network are either connected to end systems or used strictly for routing traffic through the network.

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model does circuit switching take place?
questions.explanation: Circuit switching takes place at the physical layer, involving physical links and dedicated channels.

questions.text: Which of the following is a characteristic of circuit switching?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Resources must be reserved before communication starts.

questions.explanation: In circuit switching, stations must make a reservation for resources (bandwidth, switches) before communication begins.

questions.text: In a circuit-switched network, how is a single link typically divided into multiple channels?
questions.explanation: Physical links in circuit switching are normally divided into channels using Frequency-Division Multiplexing (FDM) or Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM).

questions.text: During which phase(s) is addressing used in circuit-switched networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only during the data transfer phase

questions.explanation: Addressing is used only during the setup phase to establish the dedicated connection; once established, the data flows through the reserved path without needing further addressing.

questions.text: What is the primary reason for the low efficiency of circuit-switched networks in computer communications?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resources remain allocated even during periods of no activity.

questions.explanation: Efficiency is low because once resources are allocated for a connection, they are unavailable to others, even if no data is being sent (idle time).

questions.text: In circuit switching, the 'delay' during data transfer consists of:

choices.label: B
choices.text: Transmission time + Waiting time at switches

choices.label: C
choices.text: Setup time + Transmission time + Queuing time

questions.explanation: Once the circuit is established, there is no waiting time at each node. The delay is simply the sum of transmission time and propagation time.

questions.text: Suppose a circuit-switched network uses FDM to connect 4-kHz voice channels. If a link supports a maximum of 3 channels, what is the total bandwidth of that link?
questions.explanation: If each channel requires 4 kHz and there are 3 channels, the total bandwidth is \( 3 \times 4\text{ kHz} = 12\text{ kHz} \).

questions.text: Which network type is traditionally associated with circuit switching?
questions.explanation: The traditional telephone network (PSTN) uses the circuit-switching approach at the physical layer.

questions.text: What happens during the teardown phase of a circuit-switched connection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The routing table is updated with a new destination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A signal is sent to each switch to release the reserved resources.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The bandwidth is increased for the next session.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The sender waits for an ACK to confirm data receipt.

questions.explanation: When a party disconnects, a signal is sent to all switches involved to release the channels and resources for other users.

questions.text: True or False: In circuit switching, data is divided into fixed-size packets before transmission.
questions.explanation: In pure circuit switching, data is a continuous stream and is not packetized.

questions.text: The total delay in circuit switching includes the time to create the connection, the time to transfer data, and the time to disconnect the circuit.
questions.explanation: Total delay = Setup time + Data transfer time (transmission + propagation) + Teardown time.

questions.text: In Example 8.2, a T-1 line connects two offices with 4x8 switches. Why are four output ports 'folded' back into the input ports?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To increase the speed of the T-1 line.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To allow communication between computers in the same office.

choices.label: D
choices.text: To act as a backup in case of switch failure.

questions.explanation: Folding output ports into input ports allows the switch to route internal traffic within the same office.

questions.text: In the circuit switching setup phase, what is the 'ACK' sent by system M used for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: To acknowledge the receipt of the first data packet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: To confirm that the connection is established and the sender can start data transfer.

questions.explanation: System A only starts the data transfer phase after receiving the ACK from system M, which confirms the end-to-end path is ready.

questions.text: At which layer of the OSI model does datagram switching primarily occur in the Internet?
questions.explanation: In the Internet, packet switching using the datagram approach is performed at the network layer.

questions.text: Datagram networks are characterized as being:
questions.explanation: Datagram networks are connectionless, meaning there are no setup or teardown phases, and each packet is handled independently.

questions.text: How are resources allocated in a datagram network?
questions.explanation: In datagram networks, there is no reservation; resources are allocated on demand as packets arrive.

questions.text: The routing table in a datagram switch is based on which information?
questions.explanation: In datagram networks, switches (routers) use the destination address in the packet header to determine the output port.

questions.text: What happens to the destination address in the header of a packet as it travels through a datagram network?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is removed after the first hop.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It remains the same during the entire journey.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is encrypted by each router.

questions.explanation: The destination address remains constant in a datagram packet header from the source to the final destination.

questions.text: Why is datagram switching considered more efficient than circuit switching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it has no propagation delay.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because resources are reallocated for other packets during idle times.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because packets always follow the same path.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it uses FDM to divide links.

questions.explanation: Efficiency is high because resources are only used when packets are being transferred. If a source is idle, those resources can be used by other packets.

questions.text: In addition to transmission and propagation time, what additional delay is common in datagram networks?
questions.explanation: Because resources are not reserved, packets may have to wait in a queue at a switch if the output port is busy, leading to variable delay.

questions.text: True or False: In a datagram network, all packets from Source A to Destination B must follow the same physical path.
questions.explanation: Each packet is treated independently; if the routing table changes or traffic patterns shift, different packets of the same message may take different paths.

questions.text: Which of the following determines the size of the packets in a datagram network?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The network and the governing protocol

choices.label: D
choices.text: The number of switches in the path

questions.explanation: The network and its protocols determine whether packets are of fixed or variable size.

questions.text: Compared to circuit switching, the delay in a datagram network is usually:
questions.explanation: Due to waiting/queuing times at each switch, the overall delay in datagram networks is typically greater and more variable.

questions.text: A virtual-circuit network is described as a 'cross' between circuit switching and datagram switching because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses FDM and routing tables simultaneously.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has setup/teardown phases like circuit switching but packets carry addresses like datagram switching.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It only operates at the physical layer.

questions.explanation: Virtual-circuit networks combine the connection-oriented phases (setup/teardown) of circuit switching with the packetized data transfer of datagram networks.

questions.text: In which layer is virtual-circuit switching normally implemented in Wide Area Networks (WANs)?
questions.explanation: Switching at the data link layer in a switched WAN is normally implemented using virtual-circuit techniques.

questions.text: What does 'VCI' stand for in the context of virtual-circuit networks?
questions.explanation: VCI stands for Virtual Circuit Identifier, which is a local address used for identifying the circuit within the scope of a switch.

questions.text: What is the scope of a Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Universal scope within the entire network

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scope limited only to the end systems

questions.explanation: The VCI is a small number that has only switch scope. When a frame leaves a switch, its VCI is usually changed.

questions.text: In a virtual-circuit network, when is the global destination address used?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only during the setup phase to create the VCIs

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is never used; only VCIs are used.

questions.explanation: Global addressing is used only during the setup phase to establish the connection and fill in the VCI tables in the switches.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding packet paths in virtual-circuit networks?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Each packet can take a different path.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All packets belonging to the same source and destination travel the same path.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Path selection is done for each packet independently at every switch.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Packets do not follow a physical path; they are broadcast.

questions.explanation: Once the setup phase is complete, a fixed path is defined, and all packets for that connection follow the same route.

questions.text: In the Virtual-Circuit Setup Phase, the 'Setup Acknowledgement' frame is sent by the:
questions.explanation: The receiver sends the Setup Acknowledgement back to the sender to complete the table entries in the switches along the path.

questions.text: During the Teardown Phase in a virtual-circuit network, what action do the switches take?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They increase the VCI number for the next user.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They delete the corresponding entry from their routing/switch tables.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They restart the setup phase immediately.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They store the path for future use.

questions.explanation: Once a teardown request or confirmation is processed, switches delete the VCI mapping entries from their tables to free up resources.

questions.text: Efficiency in virtual-circuit switching is higher than circuit switching because:

choices.label: A
choices.text: It uses global addresses for every packet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Resources can be allocated on demand rather than reserved exclusively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It does not require a setup phase.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It operates at the physical layer.

questions.explanation: While VC networks have a setup phase, resource allocation can be on demand, allowing the network to be more flexible than rigid circuit switching.

questions.text: True or False: In virtual-circuit switching, packets may arrive at the destination with different delays if resource allocation is on demand.
questions.explanation: Even though all packets follow the same path, if resources are allocated on demand, packets might experience different queuing delays at switches.

questions.text: Which switching method requires the destination address to be present in every packet header throughout the entire data transfer?
questions.explanation: In Datagram switching, each packet must contain the full destination address. In VC, packets contain a VCI. In Circuit Switching, addressing is only used in setup.

questions.text: Match the technology to the phase requirement: 'No setup or teardown phases.'
questions.explanation: Datagram switching is connectionless and does not utilize setup or teardown phases.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding resource reservation in the three types of switching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Circuit: Mandatory; Datagram: Mandatory; VC: Optional

choices.label: B
choices.text: Circuit: Mandatory; Datagram: None; VC: Optional/On-demand

choices.label: C
choices.text: Circuit: Optional; Datagram: None; VC: Mandatory

questions.explanation: Circuit switching requires reservation. Datagram has no reservation. Virtual-Circuit can allocate during setup or on demand.

questions.text: Identify the component 'Waiting time at switches' as a major delay factor in:
questions.explanation: Waiting/queuing time occurs when multiple packets compete for the same resource, common in Datagram and some Virtual-Circuit implementations.

questions.text: In a datagram network, what is the 'Routing Table' based on?
questions.explanation: The routing table in a datagram network uses the destination address to decide where to send the packet next.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the path of packets in Datagram Switching?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A dedicated physical path is established.

choices.label: B
choices.text: All packets follow the same path established in setup.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Each packet is routed independently and may take different paths.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Packets are broadcast to all nodes.

questions.explanation: Since datagram switching is connectionless, each packet is routed based on the current state of routing tables, potentially taking different routes.

questions.text: If a system sends a 'Setup Request' to Switch I, including the address of System M, which switching type is being initiated?
questions.explanation: Both Circuit and Virtual-Circuit switching require a setup phase initiated by a request containing the destination address.

questions.text: When comparing the three types, which one typically provides the highest reliability for real-time constant-bit-rate voice communication?
questions.explanation: Circuit switching provides a dedicated channel with guaranteed bandwidth and minimal delay variance, making it ideal for traditional voice traffic.

questions.text: What does a 'Teardown Confirmation' frame signify in a Virtual-Circuit network?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The sender is ready to send more data.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The receiver has acknowledged the end of the connection.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The connection has failed due to error.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The VCI table has been updated with new values.

questions.explanation: It is the final step in the teardown phase where the receiver confirms it has received the request to disconnect.

questions.text: Which method is the most appropriate for bursty data traffic commonly found in computer networks?
questions.explanation: Datagram switching is highly efficient for bursty data because it does not tie up resources during idle periods.
